coredata.dc:title: A novel strategy of transition-metal doping to engineer absorption of carbon dots for near-infrared photothermal/photodynamic therapies
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Xiao-Lu
Ding, Zhao-Yang
Deng, Si-Min
Wen, Chang-Chun
Shen, Xing-Can
Jiang, Bang-Ping
Liang, Hong
coredata.dc:description: It is of extreme importance to engineer the structural characteristics of carbon dots (CDs) to satisfy their practical applications in imaging and medical treatments. Here, Cu,N-doped CDs (Cu,N-CDs) are a new class of near-infrared-(NIR) light-induced therapeutic agents that were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using EDTA·2Na and CuCl2. Specifically, CuΙΙ was located in the inner of Cu,N-CDs via N-Cu-N complexation, endowing NIR absorption for the production of heat and reactive oxygen species. The relationships between the hydrothermal temperature, NIR absorption, and intrinsic biological activity of the Cu,N-CDs were examined. The hydrothermal temperature significantly affected the latter two when the Cu content in the Cu,N-CDs was changed. The Cu,N-CDs at 180 °C were examined for cancer phototherapeutics in vitro and in vivo. The Cu,N-CDs markedly inhibited cancer via synergistic photothermal/photodynamic therapies. In addition, the Cu,N-CDs could also act as one kind of fluorescence cell-imaging agent and IR thermal imaging agent to visualize the in vitro and in vivo treatment process, respectively. Thus, the present study provides a promising new method to prepare CDs with NIR absorption feature for multifunctional cancer theranostic applications.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photothermal and photodynamic properties of Cu,N-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the NSFC ( 21671046 , 51562001 , 21502028 and 21364002 ), Guangxi NSFC ( 2017GXNSFGA198004 and 2013GXNSFGA019001 ), Guangxi Science and Technology Program ( AD17129007 ), Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources ( CMEMR2015-A02 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of Cu,N-CDs. Inset: The corresponding size distribution of Cu,N-CDs in (a). (b) DLS data for Cu,N-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Raman (a), XPS survey (b), and Cu2p (c) spectra of Cu,N-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectra of Cu,N-CDs and N-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL emission spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs + CuΙΙ, and Cu,N-CDs in water. (b) PL emission spectra of Cu,N-CDs with different excitation wavelengths from 366 to 456 nm. (c) Vis-NIR absorption spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs + CuΙΙ, and Cu,N-CDs in water. Inset: Photographs of N-CDs, N-CDs + CuΙΙ, and Cu,N-CDs dispersed in water. (d) Vis-NIR absorption spectra of EDTA2−+ CuΙΙ and Cu,N-CDs prepared at different temperatures in water, respectively. (e) The CuΙΙ content determination by ICP-MS of Cu,N-CDs prepared at different temperatures in water determination. Note: The PL emission and Vis-NIR absorption spectra are herein qualitative characterization rather than quantitative characterization. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photothermal images of Cu,N-CDs (500 μg mL−1), N-CDs (500 μg mL−1), and pure water upon exposure to an 808 nm laser (1.0 W cm−2) for various time periods. (b) Corresponding time-dependent photothermal curves of the samples in (a). (c) Temperature variations of Cu,N-CDs (500 μg mL−1) upon exposure to the 808 nm laser (1.0 W cm−2) for five cycles (10 min of irradiation for each cycle). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis spectra of DPBF + Cu,N-CDs for various irradiation times. (b) ESR spectra of TEMP + Cu,N-CDs without (red line) or with (black line) laser irradiation. (c) ESR spectra of DMPO + Cu,N-CDs without (red line) or with (black line) laser irradiation. (d) Schematic illustration of photoinduced 1O2 generation by Cu,N-CDs. Samples were irradiated with an 808 nm laser (1.0 W cm−2). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Fluorescence (left), bright-field (middle), and merged (right) images of B16 cells after co-incubation with Cu,N-CDs for 6 h. Control group: no Cu,N-CDs, 808 nm laser irradiation at 1.0 W cm−2 for 10 min. Cu,N-CDs group: Cu,N-CDs at 500 μg mL−1, no 808 nm irradiation. Cu,N-CDs + Laser group: Cu,N-CDs at 500 μg mL−1, 808 nm irradiation at 1.0 W cm−2 for 10 min. Green fluorescence represents high DCF levels, indicating high 1O2 levels that oxidized DCFH (488 nm excitation). Fluorescence intensities were proportional to the 1O2 concentration. Scale bar: 50 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Confocal fluorescence images of B16 cells after incubation with Cu,N-CDs (405 nm excitation). Scale bar: 10 μm. (b) In vitro dark and photoinduced B16 cytotoxicity from various Cu,N-CDs concentrations. (c) PDT and PTT effects as well as their synergistic effect. (d) Confocal microscopy images of living B16 cells labeled with calcein-AM (green, 488 nm excitation) and dead cells labeled with PI (red, 543 nm excitation) after treatment with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and Cu,N-CDs solutions, respectively. These images were taken before and after irradiation with the 808 nm laser (1.0 W cm−2, 10 min). Scale bar: 50 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Biodistribution of Cu in main organs, tumor tissue, and blood of female Balb/c nude mice bearing B16 melanoma tumors after intravenous administration of Cu,N-CDs (200 μL, 3 mg mL−1) at different time points. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Whole-body photothermal image of a B16 tumor-bearing mouse after injection of a Cu,N-CDs (200 μL, 3 mg mL−1) solution into the tumor and exposure to an 808 nm laser (1.0 W cm−2) for 10 min. The red circle marks the irradiation area. Points 1 and 2 indicate the locations of the tumor and surrounding tissue, respectively. (b) Photothermal images of the tumor after injection of the Cu,N-CDs solution or saline over different time periods (0–10 min). (c) Corresponding time-dependent photothermal curves of the samples shown in (a) and (b). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photographs of tumor-bearing mice on different days after different treatments. (b) Tumor growth rates after different treatments. A saline solution of Cu,N-CDs was injected into the tumors and then exposed to 808 nm laser irradiation (Cu,N-CDs + Laser, n = 5); Mice without injection of Cu,N-CDs (Laser only, n = 5), or without 808 nm laser irradiation (Cu,N-CDs only, n = 5); blank group with neither Cu,N-CDs nor 808 nm laser irradiation (Blank, n = 5). (c) Corresponding photographs of tumors in the blank group (Blank), control groups (Cu,N-CDs only and Laser only), and test group (Cu,N-CDs + Laser). (d) H&E-stained slices of mice tumors after different treatments. Scale bar: 50 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Illustration of Cu,N-CDs preparation and their PTT/PDT of mouse melanoma (B16) cells.

head.ce:title: A novel strategy of transition-metal doping to engineer absorption of carbon dots for near-infrared photothermal/photodynamic therapies

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Science, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources
School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is of extreme importance to engineer the structural characteristics of carbon dots (CDs) to satisfy their practical applications in imaging and medical treatments. Here, Cu,N-doped CDs (Cu,N-CDs) are a new class of near-infrared-(NIR) light-induced therapeutic agents that were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using EDTA·2Na and CuCl2. Specifically, CuΙΙ was located in the inner of Cu,N-CDs via N-Cu-N complexation, endowing NIR absorption for the production of heat and reactive oxygen species. The relationships between the hydrothermal temperature, NIR absorption, and intrinsic biological activity of the Cu,N-CDs were examined. The hydrothermal temperature significantly affected the latter two when the Cu content in the Cu,N-CDs was changed. The Cu,N-CDs at 180 °C were examined for cancer phototherapeutics in vitro and in vivo. The Cu,N-CDs markedly inhibited cancer via synergistic photothermal/photodynamic therapies. In addition, the Cu,N-CDs could also act as one kind of fluorescence cell-imaging agent and IR thermal imaging agent to visualize the in vitro and in vivo treatment process, respectively. Thus, the present study provides a promising new method to prepare CDs with NIR absorption feature for multifunctional cancer theranostic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have received tremendous attention because of high water solubility, inherent luminescence, facile functionalization, excellent biocompatibility, and outstanding photostability [1–12]. Potential applications in biology and medicine include bioimaging [13–17], biosensing [18], and biotherapy [19]. In particular, CD-based photoactivated theragnostics, which combines phototherapies, such as photothermal, photodynamic, or phototriggered chemo/gene therapy, with photodiagnostics, such as photoluminescence (PL) imaging, which has spatiotemporal selectivity and specificity for disease therapy [20–30]. Photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) have been at the forefront of medical and materials sciences because they are noninvasive, with negligible side effects and drug resistance, and low systemic toxicity [31–36]. Because skin, tissue, blood, and water exhibit low absorption and scattering over the 650–950 nm near-infrared-(NIR) window, phototherapeutic agents that do have tissue-penetrating absorption in this range are vital for clinical implementation [37–39]. By absorbing NIR photons, they can generate heat or reactive oxygen species (ROS) that kill harmful cells [32,33,40]. However, most of the reported CDs absorb in the ultraviolet (UV) or visible region, and only a few attempts have been made to induce strong NIR absorption [23]. To solve this issue, Wang and coworkers used conjugated polymers, such as polythiophene, phenylpropionic acid, polythiophene benzoic acid, and polythiophene derivatives, as carbon sources to prepare CDs or graphene quantum dots for NIR light-mediated PDT or synergistic PTT/PDT of cancers [22,23]. However, the absorption of these CDs and analogues ranges from 400 to 750 nm, and cannot fully satisfy the requirements of tissue-transparent NIR absorption. Hence, it is still a major challenge to synthesize CDs that fully exhibit tissue-transparent absorption for PTT, PDT, and synergistic PTT/PDT.

According to band engineering theory, doping can tune the optical properties of CDs [41–44]. For example, Lin's group used phenyldiamine as carbon and nitrogen sources to obtain N-doped CDs with three-color light emission [45]. Sun and coworkers reported that S and N heteroatoms narrowed CDs band gaps and produced red light emission [43]. Zboril et al. demonstrated that graphitic nitrogen in CDs significantly red-shifted the absorption and produced red fluorescence [46]. Despite doping heteroatoms has been verified to have a strong effect on PL behaviors of CDs, the effect on absorption behaviors is rarely involved [47]. Moreover, the heteroatoms were mainly nonmetal elements such as N [48], S [49], and P [50]. The optical effects of doped metal ions in CDs, especially absorption, is unknown in detail. Partial transition metal ions [51–54], such as CuΙΙ [51,52], NiΙΙ [53,54], and their complexes have benign NIR absorption properties. However, their toxicities severely restrict applications in phototherapy [51]. If their toxicities were reduced, they may become excellent candidates for NIR-mediated phototherapy [55]. A combination of excellent biocompatibility of CDs and NIR absorption property of transition metal ions in transition metal ion-doped CDs may be a valid method to simultaneously solve the issues of CDs and transition metal ions on phototherapeutic applications.

Building on these ideas, we herein attempt to prepare one kind of Cu,N-doped CDs (Cu,N-CDs) via one-step hydrothermal method at a suitable temperature. It is well known that the d orbits of CuΙΙ can split under the ligand field with specific geometry, generating higher degenerate eg orbits and lower degenerate t2g orbits [56]. The splitting energy, energy gap between eg and t2g, may match the energy of NIR region when proper ligand field is formed [52]. In various coordination modes of CuΙΙ, the N-Cu-N coordination is one kind of coordination mode that can lead to NIR absorption of CuΙΙ nanomaterials. The large number of N sites in Cu,N-CDs favor N-Cu-N coordination, which increases the possibility of NIR absorption. It should be mentioned that Cu,N-CDs have been previously prepared by several groups, and applied for the doping-promoted electron transfer in photoxidation reactions [57]. Nevertheless, no NIR absorption feature was observed in those already reported Cu,N-CDs [57–60]. In those studies, inadequate synthetic methods and relatively high synthetic temperatures (200–300 °C) for Cu,N-CDs resulted in the low CuΙΙ content. The CuΙΙ content in Cu,N-CDs plays an important role in the generation of NIR absorption. In this work, a more suitable temperature (180 °C) was used to prepare Cu,N-CDs in a mild hydrothermal process that avoided exfoliation of reduced Cu. Thus, a large number of CuΙΙ were retained in the Cu,N-CDs via N-Cu-N complexation, which not only preserved the excellent CDs PL, but also enabled NIR absorption over 550–900 nm. The Cu,N-CDs have the potential to become multifunctional, NIR-mediated, cancer theranostic nanoplatforms. The present work not only presents a sophisticated pathway for the preparation of metal ion-doped CDs for NIR phototherapeutic agents, but also enables a better understanding of synthetic temperature effects on transition-metal-ion-doped CDs absorption.

As illustrated in Scheme 1 , Cu,N-CDs were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method at 180 °C using an aqueous solution of EDTA·2Na and CuCl2. A control compound, N-doped CDs (N-CDs), was obtained using the same procedure in the absence of CuCl2. The morphologies and size distributions of the Cu,N-CDs were characterized with transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS). In the TEM image of Fig. 1 a, the Cu,N-CDs were well-dispersed spherical nanodots with a Gaussian distribution of diameters ranging over 2.5–4.3 nm (Fig. 1a Inset). The full width at half maximum of the fitted curve was about 1.2 nm, revealing Cu,N-CDs with a uniform size distribution. The corresponding average hydrodynamic Cu,N-CDs diameter in the DLS analysis was about 4.0 nm (Fig. 1b), in good agreement with the TEM images.

The Cu,N-CDs chemical composition and structure were analyzed with Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The Raman spectroscopy was performed to probe the carbon hybridization state. As shown in Fig. 2 a, peaks at 1386 cm−1 (D band) and 1588 cm−1 (G band) were attributed to vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in disordered carbon sp3-and sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice of a graphite cluster (sp2), respectively. The relative intensity of the disordered D band to the crystalline G band (ID/IG) was 0.78, which indicated that the Cu,N-CDs mainly had a graphite-like structure with a few disordered carbon structures. XPS survey spectra had five peaks over 200–965 eV (Fig. 2b), assigned to C1s, Cu2p, N1s, O1s, and Na KLL, respectively. The C1s, Cu2p, N1s, and O1s were characteristic elements of Cu,N-CDs. Na KLL was derived from counterion of the carboxyl group in Cu,N-CDs. The C1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into five components (Fig. S1a). Specifically, the components with 284.6 eV and 285.5 eV binding energies were attributed to graphitic carbon atoms. The other components were assigned to C−N (287.1 eV), C−O (287.5 eV), and C=O (288.3 eV). The O1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into three components with binding energies at 530.6 eV, 531.4 eV, and 534.5 eV (Fig. S1b) for C=O, C−O, and O=C−O, respectively. These results indicated the presence of C=C, C−C, C−O, C=O, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups in the Cu,N-CDs. In addition, characteristic functional groups, which were distinct from those in commonly synthesized CDs, were observed. The main peaks of Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 were observed at 932.5 and 952.5 eV, respectively, in the Cu2p spectrum (Fig. 2c), which verified the presence of CuΙΙ in the Cu,N-CDs. In the N1s spectrum (Fig. S1c), three components, corresponding to pyridinic N (398.4 eV), pyrrolic N (399.1 eV), and quaternary-like environments (401.2 eV), could be resolved. Furthermore, FT-IR spectra were acquired to characterize the chemical bonds between CuΙΙ and the Cu,N-CDs matrix. Compared with N-CDs, the amplified FT-IR spectrum over 900−1100 cm−1 had new bands at 920 cm−1 (Fig. 2d), which could be assigned to a N-Cu-N stretching vibration of the CuΙΙ complex [61]. XPS data revealed that amounts of C, N, Cu, and Na were 47.3%, 9.7%, 10.5%, and 7.3%, respectively, for the Cu,N-CDs surface. In addition, the content of Cu in Cu,N-CDs was determined to be about 6.8% using an inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), which was close to the result obtained by XPS (10.5%). The content of Cu in Cu,N-CDs was larger than those of commonly synthesized Cu,N-doped CDs [57,60]. Combining the above-mentioned results and the previous reports on Cu,N-doped CDs, the proposed structure of the Cu,N-CDs is shown in Scheme 1. It has plenty of hydrophilic groups on the surface, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. It has a graphite-like structure with a few disordered carbons. There are pyridinic and pyrrolic structures in the Cu,N-CDs, where the CuΙΙ is located in the inner of Cu,N-CDs via the N-Cu-N complexation.

After the characterization of Cu,N-CDs, we further evaluated their optical properties. In PL spectra, Cu,N-CDs had a strong peak centered at 436 nm upon 376 nm excitation, while N-CDs exhibited PL emission at 364 nm upon 300 nm excitation (Fig. 3 a). The distinct PL emissions of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs can be explained by the CuΙΙ coordination. Like most luminescent CDs, the Cu,N-CDs had an excitation-dependent PL with emission ranging over 433–513 nm for 366–456 nm excitation (Fig. 3b), because of surface states that affect the band gap. The C−O, C=O, and O=C−OH surface groups introduced trapping states with different energy levels, thereby varying CDs emission with excitation energy. The PL quantum yield (QY) for Cu,N-CDs in aqueous solution was 6.0%, using quinine sulfate as a reference (QY = 54.0% in 0.1 M H2SO4). This value is comparable to the previous reports on CDs [62,63]. The PL property of N-CDs complexed with CuΙΙ (N-CDs + CuΙΙ) was also explored under the same condition. The PL emission of N-CDs complexed with CuΙΙ (N-CDs + CuΙΙ) was almost quenched (Fig. 3a), which suggested that the coordination CuΙΙ in Cu,N-CDs differed from that in N-CDs + CuΙΙ. In Cu,N-CDs, CuΙΙ is located in the inner of N-CDs via the N−Cu−N complexation, while for N-CDs + CuΙΙ, the CuΙΙ may be located in the outer of N-CDs via the O=C−O−Cu−O−C=O complexation.

It is crucial for an ideal phototherapeutic agent to have strong optical absorption in the NIR region, which can convert light to a substantial amount of heat energy or transfer energy to the surrounding oxygen to generate ROS when exposed to NIR light. Thus, it is important to evaluate the NIR absorption properties of a phototherapeutic agent before studying its phototherapeutic characteristics. The absorption spectra of N-CDs and Cu,N-CDs in water were displayed in Fig. 3c. N-CDs had a peak at 212 nm, which was due to the n→π* transition (Fig. S2). For the Cu,N-CDs, the n→π* absorption was red shifted to 247 nm (Fig. S2), possibly because of the C=N nitrogen coordination with CuΙΙ. It was also observed that the broad 550–900 nm absorption had a 726 nm maximum corresponding to the dz 2→dx 2 −y 2 transition of square-planar CuΙΙ complexes, but this was not observed for N-CDs (Fig. 3c). In the broad absorption spectrum of N-CDs + CuΙΙ over 550–900 nm, the 737 nm maximum absorption peak was red-shifted (Fig. 3c). This proved that the NIR absorption of Cu,N-CDs was attributed to the dz 2→dx 2 −y 2 transition of the square-planar CuΙΙ complexes, and also suggested different coordination environments for CuΙΙ in Cu,N-CDs and N-CDs + CuΙΙ. Differences in N-CDs, N-CDs + CuΙΙ, and Cu,N-CDs absorptions could be seen by the naked eye, where the solutions for N-CDs to Cu,N-CDs were respectively colorless, blue, and green (Fig. 3c, inset). This feature of the Cu,N-CDs was very different from those of previously reported Cu,N-CDs [57], which were colorless or brown. To gain insight into the mechanism of the absorption differences, hydrothermal synthesis of Cu,N-CDs was performed at 150 °C and 220 °C. The Cu,N-CDs prepared at 150 °C had longer wavelength absorption relative to those prepared at 180 °C. The as-obtained Cu,N-CDs at 150 °C had an absorption that was closer to that of EDTA2−+ CuΙΙ. The Cu,N-CDs prepared at 220 °C exhibited very low NIR absorption. ICP-MS measurements revealed that when the temperature increased from 150 °C to 220 °C, the Cu content decreased from 15.5% to 2.4% (Fig. 3e) because of the increased reduction of CuΙΙ by carbon at higher temperatures and the exfoliation of reduced Cu from Cu,N-CDs. This hypothesis agrees with that previously reported by Wu and coworkers [57]. Therefore, different amounts of CuΙΙ in Cu,N-CDs resulted in distinct absorption characteristics. In our work, the reaction temperature (180 °C) was lower than those (200–300 °C) used previously; this maintained more Cu in the Cu,N-CDs prepared at 180 °C, resulting in absorption at longer wavelengths [57–60]. Moreover, it should be noted that although the Cu,N-CDs prepared at 150 °C had longer wavelength absorption, their biological toxicity was greater than that of the Cu,N-CDs prepared at 180 °C (Fig. S3). Therefore, the Cu,N-CDs prepared at 180 °C were used to further explore other properties in the following study, denoted as Cu,N-CDs. The benign optical properties of Cu,N-CDs are valuable for further biological applications. Cu,N-CDs with strong NIR absorption are well-suited as a phototherapeutic agent, which can avoid damage to blood vessels and normal tissue exposed to UV or visible light. The remarkable PL emission endows that Cu,N-CDs can be acted as an imaging agent for image-guided diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal and photodynamic properties of Cu,N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In view of its NIR absorption characteristics, an 808 nm laser was used to irradiate an aqueous solution of Cu,N-CDs at a power density of 1.0 W cm−2. Temperature variations were recorded with a thermal imaging camera. With increased exposure time, the colors of the photothermal images continuously changed from violet (low temperature) to white (high temperature) (Fig. 4 a), corresponding to a 29 °C temperature rise from 25 to 54 °C in 10 min (Fig. 4b). In contrast, the thermal signals in pure water and in a N-CDs solution exhibited no temperature rise under the same conditions, indicating no photothermal conversion. Hence, CuΙΙ in Cu,N-CDs plays an important role in the conversion of light energy into thermal energy. Moreover, after five cycles, there was very little change in the temperature increases, suggesting good photostability (Fig. 4c).

The principle for PDT implementation is the generation of cytotoxic ROS by the selective absorption of light to kill cancer cells [33]. It has been reported that some specific structures of CDs could effectively produce ROS for PDT applications under the irradiation [23,24]. To explore the capacity of Cu,N-CDs to produce ROS via light absorption, 1,3-diphenylbenzofuran (DPBF) was used as a chemical trapping agent. ROS such as 1O2 and O2 •− can react directly with DPBF to generate oxidation products, resulting in decreased UV–vis absorption [64]. The 808 nm laser (1.0 W cm−2) was used to irradiate the aqueous solution of Cu,N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the absorption centered at 418 nm gradually decreased in the presence of Cu,N-CDs and exposure to the 808 nm laser; it decreased by 0.5 after 20 min of irradiation. UV–vis spectra were also acquired for DPBF with N-CDs and non-irradiated DPBF with Cu,N-CDs, where almost no change was observed (Fig. S4). These results illustrated that Cu,N-CDs following 808 nm laser irradiation could prominently generate ROS, and the structure of N−Cu−N in Cu,N-CDs was crucial for the generation of ROS under the irradiation. Electron spin resonance (ESR) was used to further explore the nature of the as-generated ROS by the Cu,N-CDs. 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were used as 1O2 and O2 •− (or •OH) trappers, respectively. Following 808 nm laser irradiation, the ESR signal of the TEMP adduct with 1O2 (nitroxide radical) was detected (Fig. 5b, black line); there were no significant radical nitroxide signals without laser irradiation (Fig. 5b, red line). DMPO can produce DMPO−OH adducts with •OH and DMPO−OOH adducts with O2 •− [65,66]. However, no noticeable ESR signals for DMPO−OH or DMPO−OOH adducts were observed for Cu,N-CDs under 808 nm irradiation (Fig. 5c). These results indicated that 1O2, instead of O2 •− or •OH, was generated in the Cu,N-CDs aqueous solution under 808 nm laser irradiation. The ESR results and previous studies on 1O2 production [67] indicate that when the Cu,N-CDs were excited by NIR light, 1O2 was generated by energy transfer from the excited triplet state of the sensitizer to ground-state oxygen (3O2) (Fig. 5d) like conventional PDT agents [67–70], such as porphyrin [68] and phthalocyanines [69,70], and the Cu, N doping in Cu,N-CDs improved energy transfer from Cu,N-CDs to O2. To further confirm the mechanism of photoinduced 1O2 generation, we examined the PL behavior of Cu,N-CDs at the excitation wavelength of 808 nm. A weak emission peaking at 467 nm was observed (Fig. S5a). This indicated that Cu,N-CDs can reach the excited state under 808 nm laser irradiation. Additionally, analysis of the fluorescence decay kinetics revealed two exponential decays, with the longest lifetime being 6.22 ns (Fig. S5b). Referring to the previous report on the photoinduced 1O2 generation by CDs analogues [21], a lifetime on the ns level suggested the singlet state nature of the Cu,N-CDs emission. These results strongly supported the suggested mechanism of photoinduced 1O2 generation (Fig. 5d).

Production of ROS in cells is crucial to achieve PDT. The intracellular production of 1O2 by Cu,N-CDs following 808 nm laser irradiation was studied in mouse melanoma (B16) cells using 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA) as a cell-permeable non-fluorescent probe [71]. Specifically, DCFH-DA can be selectively oxidized to highly fluorescent (green) dichlorofluorescein (DCF) in the presence of 1O2. In Fig. 6 , there was intense green fluorescence within B16 cells, indicating 1O2 production by Cu,N-CDs when irradiated with 808 nm light for 10 min. In contrast, little fluorescence was observed when the cells were not irradiated or when they were exposed to irradiation without Cu,N-CDs present. These results indicate that Cu,N-CDs irradiated by NIR light produced sufficient ROS in the cells to oxidize DCFH-DA, making them promising PDT candidates.

To examine the ability of Cu,N-CDs to enter cells, cell imaging was enabled by their PL emission. As shown in Fig. 7 a, B16 cells stained with Cu,N-CDs produced intense fluorescence emission in the cytoplasm but not in the nucleus. It revealed that the Cu,N-CDs labeled only the cytoplasm and not the nucleus, similar to the results of previously reported CDs [72]. This observation illustrated not only that Cu,N-CDs can effective enter cells, but also that they can be used as a cell-imaging agent due to their remarkable fluorescence. After that, a standard 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was performed to quantitatively determine the in vitro phototherapy of Cu,N-CDs. First, the in vitro cytotoxicity of the CDs was assessed and no obvious change in cell viability was found (Fig. 7b). All cell viabilities were >80% for Cu,N-CDs concentrations ranging over 50–500 μg mL−1. Hence, the Cu,N-CDs were suitable for phototherapy. To determined their in vitro phototherapeutic effect, B16 cells were incubated with various Cu,N-CDs concentrations and then irradiated with the 808 nm laser for 10 min. As shown in Fig. 7b, cell viability was significantly reduced from 88% to 20% with increasing Cu,N-CDs concentrations over the range 50–500 μg mL−1, suggesting concentration-dependent toxicity. There were two possible factors for Cu,N-CDs toxicity upon irradiation: photothermal effects caused by heat and photodynamic effects caused by ROS. Previous reports indicated that an ice bath eliminated photothermal effects [73]. Comparative experiments were performed in the presence of the benign ROS scavenger vitamin C (VC) (PTT only) [74], which should prevent cell death induced by ROS, or by using an ice bath to maintain the cell temperature at 4 °C during laser irradiation (PDT only). Cell viability partially recovered in the presence of VC (Fig. 7c), confirming that photoinduced ROS from the Cu,N-CDs was responsible for the cell death. In Fig. 7c, partial cell death was observed when the cells were exposed to the ice bath, implying that irradiation-induced heat also contributed to the toxicity. In conclusion, both photothermal and photodynamic effects were cytotoxic and that both phototherapeutic approaches for cancer cells improve the efficiency of one another, resulting in a PTT/PDT synergy.

To visualize the phototherapeutic effect of Cu,N-CDs, confocal microscopy was performed to image the Cu,N-CDs phototherapy. calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) fluorescent dyes labeled living cells (green) and dead cells (red), respectively. B16 cells were incubated with Cu,N-CDs for 6 h, and then treated with calcein-AM and PI. In Fig. 7d, green calcein-AM fluorescence was observed in B16 cells before irradiation, and no red PI fluorescence was detected. This indicated that Cu,N-CDs had negligible cytotoxicity in the absence of NIR irradiation. Upon exposure to the 808 nm laser for 10 min, intense PI fluorescence was observed (Fig. 7d). Merging of the fluorescence images revealed that the PI fluorescence was intracellular. Therefore, upon NIR irradiation, Cu,N-CDs killed B16 cells, in agreement with the MTT assay (Fig. 7b).

Although we have proved that Cu,N-CDs exhibit negligible cytotoxicity, in vivo biological behaviors (e.g. distribution and biodegradability) of Cu,N-CDs is still unclear. Before evaluating the in vivo phototherapeutic effects of Cu,N-CDs, the in vivo distribution and biodegradation of Cu,N-CDs need to be detailedly investigated. Female Balb/c nude mice bearing B16 melanoma tumors were chosen as animal model, where a saline solution of Cu,N-CDs was tail intravenously injected. Firstly, we have performed blood analysis to examine in vivo clearance of Cu,N-CDs in blood via measuring the Cu concentration by ICP-MS after the intravenous injection. As can be seen from Fig. 8 , the content of Cu gradually decreased with prolonged time, suggesting the gradual clearance of Cu,N-CDs in blood. Besides that, we also studied the metabolic pathway of the Cu,N-CDs via measuring the Cu concentration in the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) and tumor tissue by ICP-MS, where they were surgically removed from the mice at different time intervals after the intravenous injection. As depicted in Fig. 8, the overall level of Cu content in liver, lung, kidney, and tumor increased within 6 h postinjection. Thereafter, the content of Cu gradually decreased with prolonged time. It suggested the gradual clearance of Cu,N-CDs, in according with the results obtained by blood analysis. In addition, it should be pointed out that the content of Cu did not accumulate more in the tumor during the whole stage compared with other major organs. This result revealed that tumor has no specific absorption of Cu,N-CDs, suggesting that the intravenous injection of Cu,N-CDs is not a suitable method for further in vivo phototherapy. By comparison, a relatively higher content of Cu accumulated in the liver during the whole stage. It is known that liver can behave as a common clearance pathway due to Kupffer cells that locate inside fenestrated sinusoids of liver blood vessels, which can take up and destroy foreign nanomaterials [75]. It indicates that Cu,N-CDs can be mainly metabolized by liver, and slightly metabolized by other organs. Therefore, these results illustrated that Cu,N-CDs have benign in vivo biodegradability, which are suitable for further treatment.

In vivo PTT/PDT of cancer via Cu,N-CDs was examined in B16 melanoma tumors that were established in situ with Balb/c nude mice. We primarily used Cu,N-CDs as a kind of IR thermal imaging agent to monitor the temperature dynamics in the phototherapeutic process. After a saline solution of Cu,N-CDs was injected into the melanoma tumors of the mice, an IR thermal camera was used to record temperature variations in the tumors during 808 nm laser irradiation (1.0 W cm−2). The temperature of the tumor surface rapidly increased by 24 °C in 10 min after the irradiation, whereas the temperature of the surrounding tissues increased by < 5.0 °C (Fig. 9 a and b). Thus, the increased temperature of the tumors was high enough to kill B16 cells, but low enough in the surrounding normal tissues to avert damage. As a control, tumors in mice injected with only saline exhibited no significant changes upon irradiation (Fig. 9c). Such results illustrated that laser irradiation alone could not cause an obvious increase in temperature and that Cu,N-CDs were crucial to achieve efficient photothermal conversion in the mouse.

Balb/c nude mice that had melanoma tumors were randomly divided into four different treatment groups to study in vivo anticancer phototherapy with Cu,N-CDs. In the (Cu,N-CDs + Laser) test group, a saline solution of Cu,N-CDs was injected into the tumor site, and then irradiated with the 808 nm laser (1.0 W cm−2) for 10 min. For comparison, mice without Cu,N-CDs (Laser only) and those without exposure to the 808 nm laser irradiation (Cu,N-CDs only) were control groups; untreated mice that had no Cu,N-CDs nor laser irradiation were the (Blank) group. Tumor sizes were measured every day after these treatments. The tumors of the (Cu,N-CDs + Laser) test group gradually shrank over time (Fig. 10 a and b), indicating antitumor activity. In contrast, tumors in the control and blank groups exhibited rapid growth during the experimental period. The antitumor activity could be seen visually in terms of tumor size (Fig. 10c). Hence, solely NIR laser irradiation and solely Cu,N-CDs injection did not inhibit in vivo tumor growth, in good agreement with the in vitro results (Fig. 7d). Body weight fluctuations was another indicator for estimating in vivo toxicity [76]. The body weights of the mice were recorded over a 14-day period, and, as shown in Fig. S6, no significant weight fluctuations were observed. This implied that there were no obvious toxic effects from the treatments. Tumors were also collected for histological examination using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. Almost no changes were observed in tumor tissues of the control and blank groups (Fig. 10d). However, break-down of the nuclear membrane and shrinkage of nuclei from karyorrhexis and pyknosis were found in the (Cu,N-CDs + Laser) test group, in accordance with the in vivo anticancer activities [77]. Both the in vitro and in vivo results demonstrated that combining Cu,N-CDs and NIR light was a highly effective and feasible approach for synergistic PTT/PDT for cancer. This is a typical example that the dope of metal ion into CDs is utilized to enhance NIR absorption of CDs, and further promote the effect of NIR mediated synergistic PTT/PDT of cancer.

A new class of Cu,N-CDs with characteristic absorption in the NIR region were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using EDTA·2Na and CuCl2. The hydrothermal temperature was critical for excellent NIR absorption and intrinsic biocompatibility of the Cu,N-CDs. High hydrothermal temperatures exfoliated CuΙΙ from the Cu,N-CDs. NIR absorption of the Cu,N-CDs was benifical to produce heat and reactive oxygen species, enabling cancer phototherapy. In vitro and in vivo results indicated synergistic PTT/PDT growth inhibition of melanoma tumors. In addition, Cu,N-CDs can also act as one kind of fluorescence cell-imaging agent and IR thermal imaging agent to visualize the in vitro and in vivo treatment process, respectively. It is believed that the present research not only paves the way to use metal ion-doped CDs as promising multifunctional NIR light-mediated theranostic nanoplatforms for biomedical applications, but also deepens the understanding of the influence of metal-ion on absorption characteristics of CDs.

Ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA·2Na) was obtained from Beijing InnoChem Science & Technology Co., Ltd. Cupric chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) medium (high glucose) was obtained from the Gibco Company. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was obtained from HyClone Company. 0.25% Trypsin with EDTA was obtained from the Auki Biological Company. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazolyl-2)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), penicillin streptomycin, and vitamin C (VC) were purchased from the Sigma Company. 4% paraformaldehyde fixative was purchased from the Solarbio Company. 1,3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were supplied from the Aladdin Reagent Company. 2′,7′-Dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA), propidium iodide (PI), calcein-AM was purchased from the Beyotime Company. Tetrahydrofuran (THF) and dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from the Xilong Chemical Corporation. Mouse melanoma (B16) cells were supplied from Immunetech International. Water was purified with a Milli-Q apparatus (Millipore, Bedford, MA), and was used throughout the experiments. All other chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification.

Absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV–vis spectrometer (UV-2600). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Perkin Elmer FT-IR spectrometer. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was performed with a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS-90. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEM-2100F field-emission TEM with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Raman spectra were acquired on a Renishaw in Via Raman microscope with 514 nm excitation. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained with an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with a monochromatic Al Kα (1486.6 eV) X-ray source. ESR measurements were performed with a Bruker Model A300 spectrometer at 298 K. Steady-state fluorescence spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer LS-55 luminescence spectrometer. The concentrations of CuΙΙ in all samples were determined with inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS Flexar/NexlON300X). Fluorescence decay curve was performed on Fluorescence lifetime Analyzer (HOLITA-FLA-T01).

Cu,N-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. First, a 15 mL aqueous solution of EDTA·2Na (0.1 M) and CuCl2 (0.1 M) was added to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The mixture was heated at 180 °C for 6 h. The upper green solution was filtered using a Millipore filter (0.22 μm) to remove the precipitation. The filtrate was dialyzed with a MD 31(500–1000 Da) dialysis tube for 48 h to remove remaining salts and small fragments. After freeze-drying, Cu,N-CDs were obtained.

Using the same procedure, Cu,N-CDs (150 °C) and Cu,N-CDs (220 °C) were synthesized at 150 °C and 220 °C. N-CDs were prepared in the absence of CuCl2 and later mixed with CuCl2. The mixture was dialyzed for 48 h, and then freeze-dried to obtain N-CDs + CuΙΙ.

Murine melanoma (B16) cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM) containing 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1% (v/v) 100 U mL−1 penicillin and 100 μg mL−1 streptomycin. The cells were incubated in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 at 37 °C.

Intracellular ROS generation under light irradiation was detected with DCFH-DA. B16 cells were seeded in 24-cell plates and incubated with or without Cu,N-CDs (500 μg mL−1) for 6 h. The culture medium was removed and washed twice with PBS buffer. Following incubation with DCFH-DA (10 μM) for 30 min in the dark, the cells were washed twice with PBS, and then exposed to 808 nm laser irradiation for 10 min at a power density of 1.0 W cm−2. After irradiation, fluorescence images of the treated cells were acquired with a multi-mode microplate reader (Biotek Cytation 5).

In vitro dark cytotoxicity of Cu,N-CDs was determined via a standard MTT assay using B16 cells. The cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 2 × 104 cells per well for 24 h. They were then incubated in various Cu,N-CDs concentrations (50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500 μg mL−1) for 24 h. MTT (10 μL in PBS, 5 mg mL−1) was introduced to each well and incubated for 4 h. The culture medium was then removed, and 150 μL of DMSO was added to each well. After the plate was rocked for 10 min, the cytotoxicity was tested via Infinite M1000, and the 570 nm absorbance was measured. The absorbance was also measured for an untreated cell population under the same experimental conditions, and was used as a reference to establish 100% cell viability.

The in vitro photoinduced cytotoxicity of Cu,N-CDs was determined by an MTT assay on B16 cell lines. The cells were incubated in a culture medium containing a series of Cu,N-CDs concentrations (50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500 μg mL−1) for 24 h. The medium was removed, and the cells were washed with PBS three times to remove unbound Cu,N-CDs. An 808 nm laser (1.0 W cm−2) was then used to irradiate the cells for 10 min. The cells were then incubated for additional 6 h, and their viability was measured via the MTT assay. The same procedure was performed for the in vitro Cu,N-CDs photoinduced toxicity of B16 cells in the presence of VC (2.0 mg mL−1) or at 4 °C.

Confocal microscopy (Carl Zeiss LSM 710, Jena, Germany) was also used to observe the phototherapy of Cu,N-CDs on B16 cells. Briefly, the cells were seeded into 24-well plates at a density 4 × 104 cells per well and cultured at 37 °C under a 5% CO2 atmosphere for 24 h. Then 0.5 mL of fresh medium with or without Cu,N-CDs (500 μg mL−1) was added to each well for incubation over 6 h. The medium was then discarded, and the cells were washed with PBS three times to remove unbound Cu,N-CDs. Then 1 mL of fresh medium was added again. An 808 nm laser (1.0 W cm−2) was used to irradiate the cells for 10 min. The cells were then stained with calcein-AM (1.6%) and PI (2%) for 10 min before and after irradiation, and confocal microscopy images were taken using a 20 × objective lens.

B16 cells were plated onto cover glasses in 6-well plates at a density of 2.0 × 104 cells per well and allowed to adhere for 24 h. Culture media was replaced with new DMEM media containing 1 mg mL−1 Cu,N-CDs and incubated for 6 h at 37 °C. After that, the supernatant was carefully removed and the cells were washed three times with PBS. Subsequently, the cells in each well were fixed with 1 mL of 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min at room temperature and then washed twice with PBS. The slides were examined with a confocal laser scanning microscope imaging system (Carl Zeiss LSM 710, Jena, Germany).

A saline solution of Cu,N-CDs (200 μL, 3 mg mL−1) was tail intravenously injected into Female Balb/c nude mice bearing B16 melanoma tumors. Three mice are sacrificed to give an average value for the final result. Heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, tumor tissue, and blood were removed at different time intervals (1 h, 6 h, 12 h, 1 day, 3 days, 7 days, 14 days) after injection, and the analysis of Cu content was measured by ICP-MS (Flexar/NexlON300X, Perkin-Elmer, U.S.A.).

Female Balb/c nude mice bearing B16 melanoma tumors were used as in vivo experimental models. All animal experiments were performed in compliance with the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committees (IACUC) guidelines. The tumors were generated by subcutaneous injection of 2 × 106 B16 cells on the flank region of each Balb/c nude mouse. When the volumes of the tumors increased to about 100 mm3, the Balb/c nude mice were randomly divided into four groups (n = 5 per group).

Test group (Cu,N-CDs + Laser): A saline solution of Cu,N-CDs (200 μL, 3.0 mg mL−1) was injected into the tumor site, and the mice were exposed to an 808 nm laser at a power density of 1.0 W cm−2 for 10 min. Control groups: mice without Cu,N-CDs (Laser only) or without 808 nm laser irradiation (Cu,N-CDs only). Blank group (Blank): mice without Cu,N-CDs and without 808 nm laser exposure. After 4 h post-injection, the entire region of the tumors was irradiated with an 808 nm laser (1.0 W cm−2) for 10 min. The tumors temperatures and infrared thermographic maps were monitored simultaneously using an infrared thermal imaging camera (MAG30, Magnity Electronics, China).

After treatment, the tumors sizes were monitored every day and characterized by volume= (tumor length) × (tumor width)2/2. Relative tumor volumes were calculated as V/V0, where V0 was the initial volume before treatment. The mice body weights were also monitored every day for 14 days. Five mice were used to assess the mean and standard deviation of the data.

Histology Study: Two weeks after the various treatments, the mice were sacrificed and the tumors were collected for analysis. The tumor tissue sections were stained with H&E following the standard protocol. All sections were examined under a multi-mode microplate reader using a 20 × objective lens.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the NSFC (21671046, 51562001, 21502028 and 21364002), Guangxi NSFC (2017GXNSFGA198004 and 2013GXNSFGA019001), Guangxi Science and Technology Program (AD17129007), Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (CMEMR2015-A02).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Online data Online data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.04.001.

Two-photon excitation triggers combined chemo-photothermal therapy via doped carbon nanohybrid dots for effective breast cancer treatment

Nanochemistry and nanomedicine for nanoparticle-based diagnostics and therapy
Gold nanoparticles in biomedical applications: recent advances and perspectives
Thermophysical and biological responses of gold nanoparticle laser heating
Light in diagnosis, therapy and surgery
Ultra-small Nd(3+)-doped nanoparticles as near-infrared luminescent biolabels of hemin in bacteria
Neodymium-doped NaHoF4 nanoparticles as near-infrared luminescent/T2-weighted MR dual-modal imaging agents in vivo

Luminescent colloidal carbon dots: optical properties and effects of doping [Invited]
Red emissive sulfur, nitrogen codoped carbon dots and their application in ion detection and theraonostics
Synthesis of lanthanum doped carbon dots for detection of mercury ion, multi-color imaging of cells and tissue, and bacteriostasis
Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging
Graphitic nitrogen triggers red fluorescence in carbon dots
Engineering surface states of carbon dots to achieve controllable luminescence for solid-luminescent composites and sensitive Be2+ detection

Luminescent S-doped carbon dots: an emergent architecture for multimodal applications
Non-metal single/dual doped carbon quantum dots: a general flame synthetic method and electro-catalytic properties
Cu(II) doped polyaniline nanoshuttles for multimodal tumor diagnosis and therapy
Cupreous complex-loaded chitosan nanoparticles for photothermal therapy and chemotherapy of oral epithelial carcinoma
High photothermal activity within neutral nickel dithiolene complexes derived from imidazolium-based ionic liquids
Solvent effects for spectroscopic properties of near-infrared absorbing nickel–dithiolene complex [Ni(iPr2timdt)2] (iPr2timdt: monoanion of 1,3-Diisopropylimidazolidine-2,4,5-trithione)

Cu-N dopants boost electron transfer and photooxidation reactions of carbon dots

Difunctional Cu-doped carbon dots: catalytic activity and fluorescence indication for the reduction reaction of p-nitrophenol
Facile synthesis of copper doped carbon dots and their application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe in the detection of Fe3+ions
Nanoporous polyporphyrin as adsorbent for hydrogen storage
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
White-emitting carbon dots with long alkyl-chain structure: effective inhibition of aggregation caused quenching effect for label-free imaging of latent fingerprint

Aptamer-conjugated graphene quantum dots/porphyrin derivative theranostic agent for intracellular cancer-related MicroRNA detection and fluorescence-guided photothermal/photodynamic synergetic therapy
Highly charged ruthenium(II) polypyridyl complexes as lysosome-localized photosensitizers for two-photon photodynamic therapy
NIR-II light activated photodynamic therapy with protein-capped gold nanoclusters
Physical mechanisms of generation and deactivation of singlet oxygen
One-step preparation of a water-soluble carbon nanohorn/phthalocyanine hybrid for dual-modality photothermal and photodynamic therapy
Graphene loading water-soluble phthalocyanine for dual-modality photothermal/photodynamic therapy via a one-step method
Ratiometric biosensor for aggregation-induced emission-guided precise photodynamic therapy
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Designing multi-branched gold nanoechinus for NIR light activated dual modal photodynamic and photothermal therapy in the second biological window
Cell-specific and pH-activatable rubyrin-loaded nanoparticles for highly selective near-infrared photodynamic therapy against cancer
MoO3−x quantum dots for photoacoustic imaging guided photothermal/photodynamic cancer treatment
Multifunctional Bi@PPy-PEG core-shell nanohybrids for dual-modal imaging and photothermal therapy
